text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD RE-EVALUATION / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology
PARTICIPATING SERVICES: Hepatobiliary Surgery, Colorectal Surgery, Interventional Radiology, Thoracic Surgery, Radiology, Palliative Care (consulted)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS) with bilobar hepatic and now newly documented pulmonary metastases, presenting for multidisciplinary tumor board re-evaluation following CT CAP demonstrating disease progression on maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/Avastin, with the emergence of new bilateral pulmonary nodules and a biopsy-confirmed colorectal metastasis to the right lower lobe. The purpose of this visit is to review histologic confirmation, assess overall disease burden, and formulate a second-line treatment strategy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Delgado was originally diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 10 months ago. At that time, staging CT and liver MRI revealed a 4.5 cm primary sigmoid tumor with regional nodal involvement (cT3 N2b) and synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases in four hepatic segments (largest 3.8 cm in segment VI). The tumor was confirmed to be KRAS G12D-mutant (VAF 42%), NRAS wild-type, BRAF wild-type, MSS with low TMB (3.2 mut/Mb), ruling out anti-EGFR therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitor candidacy at the outset. His initial NGS panel also identified co-mutations in APC, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 — a molecular landscape consistent with a relatively aggressive CRC phenotype.

He was enrolled in first-line treatment with FOLFOX (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus/infusional) plus bev 5 mg/kg every 2 weeks. He completed a total of 12 cycles over approximately 6 months. Radiographically, he achieved a meaningful partial response: his hepatic metastases decreased significantly (e.g., segment VI lesion from 3.8 to 2.3 cm), and CEA fell from 487 ng/mL at baseline to 87 ng/mL at the end of induction. Unfortunately, he developed progressive Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy during induction therapy. By cycle 10, neuropathy had progressed to grade 3 — bilateral distal sensory loss to the mid-calf and wrists, difficulty with fine motor tasks and ambulation on uneven ground — prompting Eloxatin discontinuation at a cumulative dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. Electromyography/nerve conduction studies confirmed a length-dependent, predominantly axonal sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy consistent with oxali injury. Gabapentin 300 mg TID was initiated with modest benefit. Cycles 11 and 12 were completed without oxali (5-FU/LV/bev only). He was also managed for bev-related hypertension throughout induction, requiring addition of amlodipine and ultimately lisinopril.

Following completion of 12 induction cycles, he transitioned to maintenance therapy with 5-FU/leucovorin/Avastin (Avastin 7.5 mg/kg) per OPTIMOX1/CAIRO3-informed strategy — preserving bev backbone to maintain disease control while allowing neuropathy recovery. During the first 2 months of maintenance (approximately months 6-8 from diagnosis), he had stable disease, CEA 79 ng/mL. However, at his most recent restaging CT CAP (approximately 4 months into maintenance, 10 months from diagnosis), there was clear disease progression: two new pulmonary nodules — right lower lobe 1.1 cm and right upper lobe 0.7 cm — new from any prior imaging. There was also slight increase in his segment VI hepatic lesion (2.5 cm, previously 2.3 cm) and a minor increase in the segment VII lesion. CEA rose to 142 ng/mL (from 79). CA 19-9 climbed to 108 U/mL (from 64 at end of induction).

A CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule was performed by interventional radiology shortly after the progression scan. Pathology returned this week: metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, morphology consistent with colorectal primary. IHC: CK20 positive, CDX2 positive, CK7 negative, TTF-1 negative, SATB2 positive. KRAS G12D confirmed by targeted PCR on biopsy tissue — concordant with the primary tumor profile. This confirms that the new pulmonary lesions represent hematogenous spread from the colorectal primary rather than a primary lung malignancy.

Mr. Delgado comes today with his wife for multidisciplinary discussion. He reports no new acute symptoms — some mild fatigue (improved compared to induction), mild persistent tingling in his feet and hands (neuropathy improving), no cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea at rest or with mild exertion. Appetite is acceptable; he has regained approximately 3 lbs since completing induction. He returned to part-time teaching approximately 1 month ago. He denies abdominal pain, change in bowel habits, hematochezia, or melena. No chest pain.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (improved from induction); weight gain of ~3 lbs over last 2 months. No fevers, no night sweats.
- Pulmonary: No cough. No hemoptysis. No dyspnea at rest or with moderate activity.
- Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain. No nausea at present. No change in bowel habits. No hematochezia or melena.
- Neurological: Residual bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, improving — grade 1-2 at this visit; tingling predominantly in feet, fine motor function improving. Gait is stable. Gabapentin providing partial relief.
- Cardiovascular: Hypertension managed on amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 10 mg; blood pressure well-controlled per recent cardiology follow-up.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, no new joint symptoms.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria, normal urinary stream.
- Psychiatric: Some anxiety about disease progression; patient and wife appear to be coping.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS) with bilobar hepatic and now pulmonary metastases — as detailed above.
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; HbA1c most recently 7.1% (approximately 2 months ago).
3. Hypertension — now requiring dual antihypertensive therapy (amlodipine, lisinopril), partially attributable to Avastin/Cyramza mechanisms.
4. Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, grade 3 at worst (now improving toward grade 1-2); EMG/NCS consistent with axonal sensorimotor neuropathy.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Mr. Delgado is a high school teacher (currently working part-time). He lives with his wife in a single-story home. He is a former smoker — quit approximately 12 years prior to diagnosis (prior cumulative history approximately 20 pack-years). He does not consume alcohol. He has two adult children who are actively involved in his care. He has a strong support network at home and at his school community. Financial toxicity has been discussed and a social work referral was placed during induction.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: colorectal cancer (diagnosed in his 60s; specific subtype and genetic workup not documented). No other known family members with colorectal cancer, Lynch syndrome-associated cancers, or BRCA-related malignancies. Germline NGS testing performed at diagnosis was negative for pathogenic variants in APC, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and MUTYH — consistent with a sporadic colorectal cancer rather than an inherited syndrome.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies. No prior reaction to contrast agents or chemotherapy agents documented.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (type 2 diabetes)
2. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily (hypertension/Avastin-related)
3. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (hypertension)
4. Gabapentin 300 mg PO TID (oxali neuropathy, partial taper underway)
5. Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN nausea (as needed)
6. Loperamide — available at home PRN (not currently needed on maintenance)
7. Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (cardiovascular risk reduction)
8. Vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Well-appearing Hispanic male appearing his stated age. Sitting comfortably in examination room accompanied by his wife.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 98.2°F, weight 176 lbs (up 3 lbs from last visit).
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx without mucositis or ulceration. No icterus.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. JVP not elevated.
Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion suggesting effusion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Mild fullness in the left lower quadrant over the sigmoid region, palpable but not tender — chronic finding consistent with known primary tumor. Liver span approximately 14 cm by percussion (top normal to mildly enlarged), without tenderness. No splenomegaly. No shifting dullness or fluid wave (no clinical ascites at this time).
Extremities: No pitting edema. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally. Skin intact.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Mild decrease in light touch and vibration sense in bilateral feet to the ankles; intact in lower calves now compared to prior exam (improving). Romberg test mildly positive. Gait steady on flat surface, cautious on tandem walk. Fine motor testing: grip strength 4+/5 bilaterally, some mild difficulty with rapid alternating movements. Consistent with improving grade 1-2 sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (recent):
- CEA: 142 ng/mL (rising from 79 ng/mL; baseline 487 ng/mL)
- CA 19-9: 108 U/mL (rising from 64 U/mL)
- CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.9 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Plt 274 x10^9/L
- CMP: Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~73 mL/min/1.73m2), AST 38 U/L, ALT 42 U/L, Alk Phos 127 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, Albumin 3.7 g/dL
- LDH: 248 U/L (mildly elevated)
- HbA1c: 7.1% (2 months prior, diabetes well-controlled)
- PT/INR: 1.1 (within normal limits)
- UGT1A1 genotyping (obtained proactively in anticipation of Camptosar use): *1/*28 heterozygous — intermediate metabolizer; standard starting iri dose appropriate with close monitoring for toxicity (grade 3-4 neutropenia and diarrhea risk modestly elevated compared to *1/*1 individuals)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Most recent CT CAP (approximately 10 months from diagnosis): Disease progression from maintenance therapy. Two new pulmonary nodules — right lower lobe 1.1 cm and right upper lobe 0.7 cm — not present on any prior imaging. Liver: segment VI lesion 2.5 cm (slightly up from 2.3 cm post-induction nadir); segment IVa 1.2 cm (stable), segment II 0.7 cm (stable), segment VII 0.5 cm (minimally up from 0.4 cm). Primary sigmoid colon mass 3.3 cm (essentially stable). No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No bone lesions.

Lung biopsy (core needle, right lower lobe): Metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary, CK20+/CDX2+/CK7-/TTF-1-/SATB2+. KRAS G12D confirmed on lung tissue.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY:

This case was presented at the weekly GI oncology multidisciplinary tumor board with representation from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and diagnostic radiology.

Surgical Perspective — Hepatobiliary Surgery: The hepatic metastases have responded well to FOLFOX/Avastin induction with a nadir at the end of 12 cycles. However, the bilobar distribution persists, and now with emerging pulmonary metastases, the tumor board is not recommending surgical resection at this time. Future liver-directed therapy (hepatic resection, ablation, or TACE) may be revisited if pulmonary disease can be controlled, but currently the dominant concern is systemic disease control.

Thoracic Surgery: The two pulmonary nodules (RLL 1.1 cm, RUL 0.7 cm) are biopsy-confirmed colorectal metastases. At this stage, surgical metachronous pulmonary metastasectomy is deferred given multisite metastatic disease (hepatic + pulmonary) and current disease trajectory. Thoracic surgery would re-evaluate in the context of future disease response.

Medical Oncology Assessment — Treatment Planning: Mr. Delgado has progressed through Eloxatin-based first-line therapy (FOLFOX/Avastin) and through maintenance 5-FU/bev. We must now consider second-line systemic options. The following framework was applied:

1. Anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux, pani): Absolutely contraindicated due to KRAS G12D mutation. Not a consideration.

2. Continued 5-FU/bev intensification: Unlikely to overcome progression; bev was continued through maintenance and disease broke through. However, the concept of continuing VEGF inhibition beyond progression (per TML/ML18147 data) remains valid — Avastin or an alternative anti-angiogenic agent can be continued with a new chemotherapy backbone.

3. FOLFIRI plus bev: Per ML18147 (TML) trial data, continuing bev beyond first progression with a change to FOLFIRI backbone is a valid strategy with modest but real OS benefit. This would maintain the anti-VEGF mechanism that the patient has been on since diagnosis.

4. FOLFIRI plus ramu: Per the RAISE trial, Camptosar/5-FU/leucovorin (FOLFIRI) plus Cyramza (Cyramza, anti-VEGFR-2 monoclonal antibody) significantly improved both PFS and OS in previously oxali-treated mCRC patients. The advantage of ramu here is a mechanistically distinct anti-angiogenic target (VEGFR-2 versus VEGF-A). This represents a change in anti-angiogenic approach.

5. KRAS G12D targeted therapies: MRTX1133 and other KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are in active clinical development but are not yet FDA-approved for colorectal cancer. Clinical trial options were discussed with the patient. He understands that KRAS G12D inhibitors are an active area of research and was offered trial referral to our thoracic/GI oncology clinical trials program. Patient has declined trial participation at this time, citing desire to remain close to home, work commitments, and preference for an established regimen with a known safety profile.

Decision: After thorough tumor board discussion and a detailed shared decision-making conversation with Mr. Delgado and his wife, the group elected to proceed with FOLFIRI plus Cyramza as second-line therapy. Rationale: (a) change in cytotoxic backbone from Eloxatin to Camptosar; (b) mechanistically distinct anti-angiogenic agent (VEGFR-2 targeting vs. prior VEGF-A targeting); (c) RAISE trial provides level 1 evidence for this combination in oxali-pretreated mCRC; (d) UGT1A1 *1/*28 status noted — patient is a heterozygous intermediate metabolizer, and standard iri starting dose (180 mg/m2) is appropriate with careful toxicity monitoring, particularly for delayed diarrhea and neutropenia.

Performance status and fitness: ECOG PS 1. He remains fit for combination chemotherapy. Labs support adequate hepatic, renal, and bone marrow reserve to proceed.

Palliative care: Outpatient palliative care was offered and accepted. This will focus on symptom management (neuropathy, fatigue, potential Camptosar-related toxicities), goals of care conversations, and psychosocial support for patient and family.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA, KRAS G12D, MSS — DISEASE PROGRESSION ON MAINTENANCE 5-FU/Avastin:
   - New biopsy-confirmed pulmonary metastases (RLL, RUL), stable-to-mildly-progressive hepatic disease. CEA rising (142 ng/mL). Primary tumor stable.
   - Disease now involves liver and lung (previously liver only). No peritoneal disease, no bone, no CNS involvement on recent imaging.
   - Surgical resection not recommended at this time given multisite metastatic involvement.
   - Plan: Initiate second-line FOLFIRI (iri 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 min day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus ramu 8 mg/kg IV day 1, every 2 weeks, per RAISE trial regimen.
   - Pre-medications: ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 8 mg IV, atropine 0.25 mg IV (prophylactic for cholinergic syndrome), lorazepam 0.5 mg IV.
   - Patient instructed on signs/symptoms of delayed diarrhea; loperamide protocol reviewed in detail.
   - UGT1A1 *1/*28 — start at standard dose, monitor CBC and diarrhea closely each cycle. Camptosar dose reduction criteria discussed.
   - Labs to be obtained every 2 weeks prior to each treatment (CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs).
   - Repeat CT CAP restaging planned after approximately 4 cycles (8 weeks) to assess response.
   - CEA and CA 19-9 to be trended with each restaging.

2. HYPERTENSION (Avastin-RELATED, GRADE 1-2, NOW CONTROLLED):
   - Currently well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg + lisinopril 10 mg.
   - Cyramza carries similar anti-angiogenic hypertension risk via VEGFR-2 mechanism.
   - Blood pressure to be checked at each treatment visit. Cardiology co-management to continue.
   - Patient educated on home BP monitoring. Target BP <140/90 mmHg.

3. oxali-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (IMPROVING, NOW GRADE 1-2):
   - Sensorimotor axonal neuropathy per EMG/NCS, currently improving with oxali cessation.
   - Gabapentin 300 mg TID — continue; consider taper as tolerated given improvement trajectory.
   - FOLFIRI does not contain Eloxatin; no further neurotoxic Eloxatin exposure anticipated.
   - Continue monitoring; formal neurology follow-up as needed.

4. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
   - HbA1c 7.1%, well controlled on metformin.
   - Dexamethasone pre-medication with each FOLFIRI cycle may transiently worsen glycemic control — patient instructed to monitor blood glucose more closely on treatment days and day after treatment.
   - No dose changes to metformin at this time.

5. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL:
   - Patient is ECOG PS 1, working part-time, and engaged in active treatment with curative/prolongation intent clearly stated.
   - Patient has declining but still adequate performance status; disease has spread beyond liver to lungs — trajectory and prognosis discussed in frank but compassionate terms.
   - Palliative care involvement initiated for proactive symptom management and advance care planning discussions. First outpatient palliative care appointment scheduled.
   - Social work referral previously placed; follow-up to assess any ongoing financial or support needs.

Return to clinic: 2 weeks for cycle 1, day 1 of FOLFIRI/ramu. Patient to call with any fever >38.0°C, intractable diarrhea, severe mucositis, unusual bleeding, or significant blood pressure elevation.

Time spent with patient and family in multidisciplinary counseling: 45 minutes.
Patient verbalized understanding of treatment plan, side effects, and monitoring plan. Written treatment summary provided.

Electronically signed,
Attending, Medical Oncology
